title: Green woodwork: a primer
subtitle: What green wood is, why it matters, and how to start
excerpt: Green woodwork is the practice of working timber that still holds its growing moisture; the tradition behind every riven tool handle, turned chair leg, and wattle fence panel made before machinery. This primer covers wood state, the key species, the two working modes (riving and carving), and what to start with.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for green-wood working methods and species properties; traditional British coppice practice.

content.text: Every tool handle, chair leg, rake head, and fence stake made before the industrial era was produced from
green wood

; timber worked when it still holds the water it grew with. The reason is practical: green wood splits predictably along the grain, shaves easily under the drawknife, and responds to hand tools in ways that seasoned timber does not. The craft tradition that built around these properties is called green woodwork, and most of it can be practised with a handful of simple tools.
Why green wood works differently

Timber at 40 to 80 percent moisture content is elastic and cuts cleanly with minimal force. The cells have not yet collapsed and bonded together as they do during drying. This means that a sharp knife or drawknife removes a shaving cleanly rather than crushing it; a froe splits the wood along the grain without tearing; a gouge driven by a mallet takes deep, clean cuts. The same piece of oak that requires power tools to work at 12 percent moisture can be shaped in an afternoon at 60 percent. The trade-off is that green pieces must dry before they are stable as finished objects; the drying time depends on thickness and species.
The key species
Ash splits and shaves predictably and is the traditional British handle wood for axes, hammers, and farm tools. Hazel; typically harvested as
coppice

rods; is straight-grained and easy to rive; the standard material for bean poles, hurdle stakes, and basketry uprights. Oak is dense and harder to work than ash or hazel but produces the longest-lasting pieces in outdoor use; fence posts, mallet heads, gate hardware. Sycamore and cherry are the preferred green woods for spoon and bowl carving: both split evenly along the grain and respond well to hook knives and gouges. Alder is traditionally used for clogs and small turned items; it is the most water-resistant British hardwood and stays sound in permanently wet conditions.
The two working modes
Green woodwork divides into two modes. The first is riving: using a
froe

and beetle mallet to split the wood along its grain into staves, blanks, and boards. Rived timber follows the grain; sawn timber cuts across it. A rived tool handle does not break along the grain at the point of maximum flex; a sawn one can. The second mode is carving: using the drawknife, hook knife, or gouge to remove material from a rived blank to produce a finished form; a spoon, a bowl, a chair leg, a peg.
The
drawknife

is the primary shaping tool in riving-mode green woodwork. Mounted on a shaving horse; a low, foot-operated workholding bench; the drawknife removes large amounts of material rapidly with the pull stroke. Most rived green-wood projects move through three stages: froe split to produce the blank, side axe to remove bulk waste, drawknife on the shaving horse for final shaping.
When to cut, when to use

Cut coppice and pole wood in winter (November to March), when the sap is down. The bark stays put during riving. Summer-cut wood has more free moisture and is softer just after cutting. The bark can bond to the surface in the first few drying days. Work spoon and bowl blanks within a week of felling. For tool handles and posts that fit into metal heads, let the wood part-dry for 3 to 4 weeks before fitting. The joint tightens as the wood finishes drying. For turning on a pole lathe, work the blank as green as you can. Green wood turns more cleanly than part-dried.
Where to start

A green ash or sycamore spoon is the standard first piece. Rive a fresh log in half. Use the half-round stave as a spoon blank. Carve with a sloyd knife and a hook knife. One afternoon makes a usable spoon. The piece shows every principle of green woodwork: grain direction, the way cutting eases when the wood is wet, and how the blank shape feeds into the finished form. Tool handles and bean poles are the next step once the spoon is done.
